planting.note: Place outdoors in a shade to bright indirect light location after temperatures remain above 60°F; in zone 9 place outdoors April–October. Thai Constellation is a tissue-culture selection that produces consistent, stable cream-and-green variegation on every leaf — unlike naturally occurring variegated Monstera (var. albo-variegata) which can revert to solid green. Protect from any direct sun, which causes permanent bleaching of the white variegated areas. Provide a moss pole or other climbing support; Monstera naturally climbs by aerial roots in its native forest understory. As the plant matures, leaf fenestrations (the characteristic holes and splits) develop and become progressively larger and more elaborate on successive leaves.

directions: Plant in shade to bright indirect light — never in direct sun, which irreversibly bleaches and kills the pale variegated areas of the leaf — in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist soil; Thai Constellation is a variegated cultivar of Swiss Cheese Plant (Monstera deliciosa) that was developed through tissue culture in Thailand and produces spectacular cream-and-green marbled variegation on the large, deeply fenestrated (perforated and split) leaves — creating one of the most visually dramatic and internationally collectible foliage plants in the contemporary indoor and outdoor tropical plant market. The variegation pattern is unique on each leaf: irregular cream-white to yellow-white splashes, sectors, and marbling on the dark green background create a different constellation of pale color on every leaf — never exactly the same twice — which explains the cultivar name. The characteristic Monstera leaf fenestrations (windows and splits in the large leaves) combined with the cream-and-green variegation creates a leaf form and color pattern that has become one of the most recognized symbols of contemporary tropical plant culture and indoor garden design. As an outdoor shade container plant for zone 9's warm season, Thai Constellation provides an extraordinary level of foliage sophistication on covered patios and shaded garden areas.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Monstera deliciosa for Florida Indoor and Outdoor Use
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Variegated Tropical Foliage for Gulf South Patios
American Horticultural Society — Monstera Cultivar and Variety Guide

planting.note: Plant container-grown camellias in fall (September–November in zone 9); fall planting allows root establishment before the winter-spring bloom season. Camellias strongly prefer acidic soil (pH 5.5–6.5); test and amend soil if needed before planting. Do not plant too deeply — the crown should be at or slightly above soil level. In zone 9 camellias bloom October through March, with peak flowering December–February — providing the only large, colorful blooms in the landscape during the coldest months. Avoid planting in locations with afternoon western sun, which can cause petal browning on open flowers in warm spells.

directions: Plant in partial shade to filtered light — camellias prefer the bright, indirect light of open shade under tall pines or high-branched trees, avoiding deep shade or full afternoon sun; White by the Gate is a registered Camellia japonica cultivar producing elegant white flowers in the formal double or peony form that represents one of the most refined and sophisticated floral displays available from any landscape shrub in zone 9's winter garden. The pure white flowers — whether formal double, semi-double, or peony form — provide a clean, luminous, refined contrast to the dark, glossy, evergreen foliage of the camellia shrub, creating a winter display of formal elegance that has made white camellias a staple of Southern estate and garden district plantings for generations. Camellia japonica is one of the defining landscape plants of the American Gulf South — thriving in the same partial shade, acidic, well-drained conditions that characterize the longleaf pine and mixed hardwood forests native to the region. The combination of large (3–6 inch) formal flowers in January–March when nothing else of comparable size or quality blooms in the landscape, glossy evergreen foliage year-round, and the exceptional longevity of well-sited camellias (specimens over 100 years old are documented in the Gulf South) makes camellia japonica cultivars essentially irreplaceable in zone 7–9 winter garden design.
sources: American Camellia Society — Camellia japonica Cultivar Registry
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Camellia Culture for Gulf South
University of Georgia Extension — Camellia Japonica Selection for Southern Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 sow March–May or September for a fall planting. Summer Jewel Red is an All-America Selections award-winning selection of Texas Sage (Salvia coccinea), a native annual of the Gulf Coast and Southeast US that naturalizes prolifically in warm gardens. In zone 9 it self-seeds abundantly — allowing one generation of plants to set seed ensures volunteers in subsequent years. The compact form (18–24 inches) blooms 3–4 weeks earlier from seed than standard-sized Salvia coccinea. The vivid red tubular flowers are among the most reliable hummingbird plants available for zone 9 summer gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Summer Jewel Red Salvia is drought tolerant once established and blooms most freely in full sun but performs well in partial shade in zone 9; Summer Jewel Red is an All-America Selections award-winning compact cultivar of Tropical Sage (Salvia coccinea), a native annual wildflower of the Gulf Coast, Caribbean, and Central America that produces bright red, tubular flowers organized in whorled spikes on upright, hairy stems. The compact selection (18–24 inches) was specifically bred from a species that normally reaches 3–4 feet, creating a more garden-appropriate size while maintaining the free-flowering, heat-tolerant, hummingbird-attractive character of the wild species. Salvia coccinea is one of the most effective hummingbird plants available for zone 9 — the vivid red, narrow tubular flowers are precisely adapted for hummingbird pollination and attract Ruby-throated and other hummingbirds with unusual reliability wherever the plant is grown in their range. As a native species, Tropical Sage supports native bees and hummingbirds while naturalizing through self-seeding to create stable, self-renewing populations in zone 9 gardens once established. The combination of hummingbird value, heat tolerance, shade adaptability, self-seeding naturalization, and low maintenance needs makes it one of the most practical ornamental annuals for Gulf South summer gardens.
sources: All-America Selections — Salvia Summer Jewel Red Award
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Salvia coccinea Native Plant
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Salvia coccinea for Florida Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April or start from seed August–September for fall bloom. Arizona Sun is an All-America Selections winner selected for compact, non-flopping form (8–12 inches rather than the 18–24 inch height of standard Gaillardia x grandiflora). The compact form is more floriferous and longer-lasting in garden performance than standard selections. Gaillardia requires excellent drainage — it is extremely susceptible to root rot in wet, heavy soils. Deadhead to extend bloom; allow some heads to mature for seed if naturalization is desired. In zone 9 it blooms most heavily in spring (March–May) and fall (October–November).

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil — Arizona Sun Blanket Flower demands the same excellent drainage and lean, dry conditions as its prairie native parent species; Arizona Sun is an All-America Selections award-winning compact selection of Blanket Flower (Gaillardia x grandiflora), producing the classic Gaillardia flower — a large, single, daisy-form flower with bright red to orange-red ray petals tipped with yellow, surrounding a prominent rust-red central dome of disc florets — at a compact, non-flopping scale that makes it practical for the front of sunny borders, container culture, and mass planting. The vivid red-and-yellow bicolor combination of Gaillardia flowers is one of the most intense and saturated warm-color combinations available from any perennial flower, creating a bold, cheerful display through the spring and fall bloom seasons in zone 9. Gaillardia's native prairie origin (G. pulchella is native across much of the Central and Southern US) means natural adaptation to the lean, dry, hot conditions of zone 9 summers — thriving in conditions that would stress or kill less drought-adapted perennials. The combination of vivid color, native adaptation, extreme drought tolerance, and compact habit makes Arizona Sun one of the most useful and reliable compact perennials for sunny, dry zone 9 situations.
sources: All-America Selections — Gaillardia Arizona Sun Award
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Gaillardia x grandiflora
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Blanket Flower for Florida Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow in full sun after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 sow March–May. Fiesta del Sol is a compact selection (2–3 feet) of Mexican Sunflower — dramatically smaller than the standard species (5–6 feet), making it practical for mixed borders and small garden spaces where the standard type would overwhelm surrounding plants. The compact size was the primary selection criterion for this All-America Selections winner. Despite the smaller size, Fiesta del Sol produces the same vivid orange flowers as the standard type, beginning to bloom earlier from seed (55–70 days vs 80–90 days for taller selections). Self-seeding occurs in warm conditions; allow a few plants to go to seed in zone 9 for volunteer seedlings in subsequent seasons.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Fiesta del Sol Tithonia thrives in the heat and lean conditions that reduce the excessive vegetative growth and delayed flowering of overfertilized plants; Fiesta del Sol is an All-America Selections award-winning compact cultivar of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia), bred specifically to reduce the scale of this Mexican native from its typical 5–6 foot height to a more garden-friendly 2–3 feet while retaining the vivid, large (3–4 inch), brilliant orange-scarlet flowers and the exceptional heat, humidity, and drought tolerance that make Tithonia outstanding for zone 9 summer gardens. The vivid orange-scarlet flowers are among the most intensely colored of all summer annuals — a warm, saturated orange that is particularly effective in late-summer and fall garden compositions with purple, blue, and cream companions. Tithonia is one of the premier butterfly and pollinator plants available — the large, nectar-rich flowers attract swallowtails, fritillaries, monarchs, and large sulphur butterflies in significant numbers throughout the bloom season. The drought tolerance and heat performance of Tithonia in the peak of Gulf South summer (July–September) when many annuals struggle to maintain quality makes it one of the most reliable high-color, high-pollinator-value summer plants available for zone 9.
sources: All-America Selections — Tithonia Fiesta del Sol Award
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Mexican Sunflower for Florida Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tithonia for Gulf Coast Butterfly Gardens
